researchFindings.claim: Perform chest compressions at a rate of 100 to 120 per minute on the lower half of the sternum (center of the chest, between the nipples)
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core compression rate and positioning — stepByStepActions

researchFindings.claim: Compress to a depth of at least 2 inches (5 cm) but no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm) for an average adult
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC

researchFindings.claim: Allow the chest to fully recoil (come all the way back up) after each compression; do not lean on the chest between compressions
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR

researchFindings.claim: Minimize pauses in compressions; limit any interruption to less than 10 seconds
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR

researchFindings.claim: For untrained bystanders or those unwilling/unable to give rescue breaths: perform Hands-Only CPR — continuous chest compressions with no rescue breaths. This is as effective as CPR with breaths in the first few minutes for adult cardiac arrest.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC

researchFindings.claim: For trained rescuers: perform 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths (30:2 cycle). Each rescue breath should last about 1 second and make the chest visibly rise. Pinch the nose closed and make a seal over the mouth.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR

researchFindings.claim: Gasping or irregular breathing is NOT normal breathing and should NOT delay starting CPR. Begin CPR if the person is unresponsive and not breathing normally.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Trigger condition for starting CPR — warnings / when to use

researchFindings.claim: Use an AED as soon as one is available. Turn it on, place pads on bare dry skin, follow voice prompts. Clear the patient during analysis and before a shock. Resume compressions immediately after the shock or if no shock is advised.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR

researchFindings.claim: Immediate CPR can double or triple survival rates after cardiac arrest. Without CPR, survival odds decrease by approximately 7–10% per minute.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC

researchFindings.claim: Rib fractures are a known and acceptable complication of properly performed CPR in approximately 1 in 3 cases. Do not stop CPR because of cracking sounds — imperfect CPR is far better than no CPR.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) complications encountered in forensic autopsy cases
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Warnings — rib fractures are expected, should not cause hesitation

researchFindings.claim: Over-inflating rescue breaths causes air to enter the stomach, increasing risk of regurgitation and airway obstruction. Give just enough air to make the chest visibly rise — about 1 second per breath.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: CPR Steps — How to Perform CPR

researchFindings.claim: For children (1 year to puberty): compress to about 2 inches or one-third the front-to-back chest depth. Rescue breaths are more important in children because cardiac arrest in children is usually caused by breathing failure, not heart rhythm problems. Use 30:2 ratio for single rescuer, 15:2 for two rescuers.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 6: Pediatric Basic Life Support: 2025 AHA/AAP Guidelines for CPR and ECC
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: American Heart Association / American Academy of Pediatrics

researchFindings.claim: Call 911 first (or have a bystander call) before starting CPR on adults. Put the phone on speaker. For a child found unresponsive (not witnessed collapse), perform 2 minutes of CPR first, then call 911 if alone.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC

suggestedSubGuides.reason: Infant CPR (under 1 year) uses two-finger or two-thumb technique, shallower depth (1.5 inches), and different hand placement. Distinct enough to warrant a separate guide to avoid confusion in an emergency.

conflictsOrWeakClaims.claim: Tilt the head back / lift the chin to open the airway before rescue breaths
conflictsOrWeakClaims.issue: Head-tilt chin-lift is standard for trained rescuers performing 30:2 CPR. For Hands-Only CPR (no rescue breaths) it is not needed and not recommended. Including it in the primary action card steps would add unnecessary complexity for untrained bystanders.
conflictsOrWeakClaims.sourceA: AHA 2025 Adult BLS (trained rescuers: head-tilt chin-lift recommended)
conflictsOrWeakClaims.sourceB: AHA Hands-Only CPR (untrained: no airway maneuver needed)
conflictsOrWeakClaims.resolution: Include head-tilt chin-lift only in the trained-rescuer rescue breath note, not in the primary step sequence.

identifiedGaps.impact: Bystanders may not know when it is safe to stop. AHA guidance: stop when EMS takes over, an AED advises and person shows signs of life, or you are physically unable to continue.
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Include a brief note in warnings or redFlags about when to stop and hand off to EMS.

recommendation: Write a new action_card in medical_safety / Airway Emergencies. Scope: adult victim (adolescent and older). Primary action: Hands-Only CPR (compression-only) for untrained bystanders. Trained-rescuer 30:2 rescue breaths as a supporting note. AED use integrated as a step. Explicitly note that gasping ≠ breathing, rib cracking is expected and should not stop compressions, and that children need rescue breaths. Do not scope to infant CPR — recommend a separate guide. Sources are AHA 2025 and Red Cross — sourceQuality: strong.
